query: Williams syndrome association with obesity mental retardation precocious puberty

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Genetic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Prader Willi syndrome: Early growth faltering followed by hyperphagia and increased weight gain by 2 to 3 years. The mild or moderate cognitive deficit, microcephaly, short stature, hypotonia, almond-shaped eyes, high-arched palate, narrow hands/feet, delayed puberty are common features. Alstrom syndrome: Blindness, deafness, acanthosis nigricans, chronic nephropathy, type 2 diabetes, cirrhosis, primary hypogonadism in males, and normal cognition are common features in Alstrom syndrome. Bardet Biedl syndrome: Intellectual disability, hypotonia, retinitis pigmentosa, polydactyly, hypogonadism, glucose intolerance, deafness, and renal disease are the features in Bardet Biedl syndrome. Other syndromes include Beckwith-Weideman syndrome and Cohen syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Assessment and Diagnosis of Metabolic and Biomechanical Complications in Obesity -- Function -- Clinical implications of altered pubertal timing
retrieved_docs.content: Altered pubertal timing in children and adolescents with obesity presents significant clinical concerns (see Table 9 ). Early puberty increases the risk of behavioral problems, mood disorders, and earlier initiation of sexual activity. In contrast, delayed puberty may lead to psychological distress and reduced bone mineral density during critical developmental periods. These risks highlight the importance of careful growth and pubertal monitoring, with timely referral to pediatric endocrinology for further evaluation when abnormalities are detected. Other reproductive complications include:

retrieved_docs.title: Prader-Willi Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Girls are born with hypoplasia of the external genitalia. Puberty usually begins at the typical age, but breast development often lags; spontaneous menarche is rare, typically occurring around age 20. Estrogen and luteinizing hormone levels are low to normal, while follicle-stimulating hormone levels are normal to high, indicating central and primary defects. Both males and females are considered infertile, and there is a notable incidence of premature pubarche in both sexes, apparently independent of obesity and insulin resistance. [16] Approximately 14% to 30% of cases are associated with advanced bone age. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothalamic Dysfunction -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Children with hypothalamic dysfunction will present short stature, obesity, hypothermia, hypodipsia, developmental delay, generalized seizures, gelastic seizures, and delayed puberty, although sometimes they have precocious puberty.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Issues of Concern -- Other Complex Syndromes Requiring Referral
retrieved_docs.content: Wilson-Turner syndrome : Patients present with X-linked intellectual disability, obesity, distinctive facial features, and behavioral abnormalities. Optimal care requires consultation with genetics and neurology.

retrieved_docs.title: Special Populations and Long-Term Management in Obesity Medicine -- Function -- Genetic obesity populations
retrieved_docs.content: Recognition of genetic obesity syndromes is crucial for making appropriate referrals and effectively treating patients with these complex conditions. Please refer to StatPearls' companion resource, " Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management ," for additional information. Rare genetic conditions associated with obesity include leptin deficiency, proopiomelanocortin deficiency, and Prader-Willi syndrome. For certain genetic disorders, therapies such as setmelanotide are available, making a precise diagnosis essential for effective treatment. Red flags for genetic obesity syndromes include: Early-onset severe obesity (BMI >95th percentile before age 5) Hyperphagia with lack of satiety Developmental delays or intellectual disability Multiple family members with severe obesity Distinctive facial features or other dysmorphisms Endocrine abnormalities (eg, hypothyroidism and hypogonadism)

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Complete leptin deficiency is a rare autosomal recessive disorder that causes severe obesity, impaired satiety, intense hyperphagia, persistent food-seeking behavior, and recurrent bacterial infections. This condition also leads to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, which results in delayed puberty.

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Assessment and Diagnosis of Metabolic and Biomechanical Complications in Obesity -- Function -- Pubertal disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Obesity significantly affects pubertal timing and development, with important implications for growth, bone health, and psychological well-being (see Table 8 ).

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Issues of Concern -- Other Complex Syndromes Requiring Referral
retrieved_docs.content: Various additional syndromes present with obesity, accompanied by additional complex signs and symptoms that warrant specialized evaluation and targeted management. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Delayed Puberty -- Differential Diagnosis -- Delayed Puberty in Females
retrieved_docs.content: Constitutional delay in puberty and growth Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism Acquired Chronic illness: cystic fibrosis, sickle cell anemia, celiac disease, etc. Psychosocial: anorexia nervosa, excessive exercise, depression, anxiety Genetic: Kallman syndrome Brain mass or tumor Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism Acquired Radiation therapy Surgery to ovaries Genetic Autoimmune ovarian failure Turner syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Delayed Puberty -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Development: Has the patient missed any milestones or been diagnosed with any developmental delay? Disorders such as Klinefelter syndrome is commonly associated with developmental delay or behavioral issues.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity in Pediatric Patients -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperphagia, atypical facial features, and neurodevelopmental delays accompanied by obesity may indicate genetic disorders. DNA methylation studies can confirm PWS. Ocular abnormalities, such as retinal dystrophy and nystagmus, are associated with BBS and Alstrom syndrome and warrant further investigation. Developmental delays in the presence of severe obesity should raise suspicion for rare conditions, such as congenital leptin or proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 1 (PCSK1) deficiencies, which may be identified by measuring serum leptin, proinsulin, and insulin levels. [38] MC4R deficiency is a cause of early-onset pediatric obesity and is diagnosed through molecular genetic studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic and syndromic causes of obesity represent a clinically significant subset of severe obesity that is often underrecognized in practice. These include monogenic disorders, such as leptin deficiency and melanocortin 4 receptor deficiency, as well as syndromic conditions like Prader-Willi syndrome and Bardet-Biedl syndrome. Patients frequently present with early-onset obesity, hyperphagia, dysmorphic features, or developmental delays that are easily overlooked during standard assessments. Delayed recognition leads to missed opportunities for early intervention and access to precision therapies. Advances in molecular diagnostics now allow more accurate characterization of these disorders. Still, many clinicians lack familiarity with the key red flags, testing strategies, and therapeutic pathways needed for timely diagnosis and management. Early recognition of genetic etiologies is essential for guiding appropriate care and improving long-term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Faciocutaneoskeletal Syndrome (Costello Syndrome) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Endocrine features Growth hormone deficiency (partial or complete) Cortisol deficiency Short stature Delayed bone age Delayed/dysregulated puberty Neonatal hyperinsulinism

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Introduction -- Red Flags for Genetic Obesity
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should request a referral for a genetic evaluation if any of the following red flag indicators are present in patients with obesity: Severe obesity onset before age 5 years Hyperphagia with food-seeking behaviors Obesity accompanied by developmental delays Obesity with distinctive dysmorphic features A family history of severe early-onset obesity Obesity and endocrine abnormalities (eg, hypogonadism and growth hormone deficiency) Obesity and vision problems or polydactyly A failure to respond to standard weight management interventions

retrieved_docs.title: Delayed Puberty -- Differential Diagnosis -- Delayed Puberty in Males
retrieved_docs.content: Constitutional delay of puberty and growth Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism Acquired Chronic illness: cystic fibrosis, sickle cell anemia, celiac disease, etc. Psychosocial: anxiety, depression Genetic Kallman syndrome Brain mass or tumor Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism Acquired Radiation therapy Testicular surgery Genetic Klinefelter syndrome Testicular regression syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: are recognized components of the broader newborn disorder grouping. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and classificatory framework in which perinatal conditions, specific respiratory/cardiovascular disorders, growth‑restriction syndromes, low‑birth‑weight classifications, and perinatal infections are interrelated, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated care for infants affected by these overlapping medical issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.
